next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 10

Harry had said one o'clock. Carmen had asked for the meeting three days after the survey — three days after she sat in her car unable to drive, feeling the specific weight of what she'd told the state surveyor settle into a place she couldn't reach to remove it. She'd found Harry alone in the TV room during evening med pass, leaned against the doorframe while Lola pretended to watch a game show behind him, and said, "Show me all of it." Harry had said one o'clock. Nothing else.

So at one o'clock she walked the length of Wing A past Devon sleeping at the nurse's station with his phone face-down on his chest, past Room 110 where Mrs. Rowan was making the low steady sound that Carmen's brain had reclassified as building noise months ago, and pushed open the TV room door.

Harry sat in his wheelchair at the card table — the one the activities aide used for bingo on Tuesdays — wearing the faded blue cardigan he wore to every after-midnight meeting Carmen had attended. Agnes sat across from him. Lola was at her post by the hallway door, one hand resting on the gold crucifix at her throat, her other hand still in the tremor that worsened at night. Earl sat in the vinyl recliner by the far window with his eyes closed, though Carmen could tell from the set of his shoulders that he was listening. Between Harry and Agnes, spread flat under the fluorescent tube that buzzed from a loose ballast, were Agnes's notebooks.

There were four of them. Composition books, the black-and-white marbled kind you could buy at CVS for a dollar nineteen, and Agnes had filled them in hand...
open_hooks_to_carry: Cliff's administrative panic — fresh flowers, redone documentation, motivational poster
Harry's Oscar-caliber confused-old-man performance with elaborate, internally consistent wrong answers

payoffs_to_land.description: The count discrepancies documented under Carmen's name become the specific thing she must explain to the state surveyor, forcing her to lie

prev_tail_excerpt:
..., one larger, one smaller, both white. She looked at Agnes.

"Can you tell which is which?" Carmen asked. "Without the packaging."

Agnes glanced at the cup. "The round one, seven millimeters, scored on one side, is the atorvastatin, ten milligrams, generic. The smaller tablet, unscored, slight bevel on the edge, is the aspirin, eighty-one milligrams. The atorvastatin has a manufacturer's imprint you cannot see from this angle."

Carmen separated the aspirin from the statin and held it out. Agnes took it and washed it down with the water. The statin remained on the tray.

"I'll chart that you took both," Carmen said.

"I know."

Carmen stood. She picked up the clipboard and the cup with the remaining pill and moved toward the door. She stopped with her hand on the handle.

"When's the next meeting?"

"Thursday. One in the morning. Harry will expect you."

Carmen opened the door and stepped into the hallway, and her posture reassembled itself: shoulders settling into the practiced ease of a nurse moving between rooms, face smoothing into professional composure. Agnes watched the shift and recognized it. She performed her own version every day. The difference was that Carmen's performance was a choice she could walk away from. Agnes's was not.

She turned back to the window. The parking lot lights made orange pools on the asphalt. The sugar maples were dark shapes against a darker sky, their October color invisible now, just mass and outline. Behind her, Margaret's breathing cycled: the click, the pause, the drain. Nine seconds. Agnes sat in the chair and waited for Thursday.
state_deltas_required: state health department survey forces maximum impairment performance while Carmen must explain medication discrepancies under her name
Carmen actively lies to a government official to protect the ring; she is legally complicit and cannot return to innocent bystander
